era_context: The Investiture Controversy is escalating in Europe; Pope Gregory VII and Emperor Henry IV are on a collision course. The Norman Conquest of England is 9 years past. In the Islamic world, the Seljuk Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Levant. The Northern Song Dynasty continues to rule China under Emperor Shenzong, with Wang Anshi's reforms in full effect. Japan remains in the Heian period.

events.title: Pope Gregory VII excommunicates King Henry IV over investiture
events.region: Holy Roman Empire and Papal States
events.description: In 1075, Pope Gregory VII excommunicated King Henry IV of the Salian dynasty for his continued practice of investing bishops without papal consent. This was the first major excommunication in what would become the Investiture Controversy. Gregory VII had issued the Dictatus Papae in 1075, asserting papal supremacy over secular rulers in ecclesiastical matters. Henry IV's response was defiant—he claimed the right to appoint bishops as part of his royal prerogative. This excommunication was a watershed moment: it demonstrated the papacy's willingness to use its spiritual authority as a political weapon and forced Henry to choose between submission and defiance.
key_figures: Cardinal Humbert (papal legate, died 1061 but his legacy influenced Gregory)

sources.name: Register of Pope Gregory VII (papal records)
Bonizo of Sutri, Liber ad amicum (contemporary polemical account)

events.certainty_note: The excommunication is documented in Gregory VII's own register and multiple contemporary chronicles. The date is well-established.

events.title: Pope Gregory VII issues the Dictatus Papae
events.description: In 1075, Pope Gregory VII issued the Dictatus Papae, a document asserting the supreme authority of the pope over all secular rulers and the church. The document contained 27 propositions, including claims that the pope could depose emperors, that no one could judge the pope, and that the pope's legates took precedence over all bishops. The Dictatus Papae was revolutionary for its time and became the ideological foundation for the Investiture Controversy. It represented a dramatic assertion of papal power and a challenge to the traditional balance between church and state in medieval Europe.

sources.name: Register of Pope Gregory VII (papal records)
Historiographic consensus on the Dictatus Papae

events.certainty_note: The Dictatus Papae is preserved in Gregory VII's register and is one of the most important documents of medieval church history. Its date and content are well-established.

events.title: Seljuk Sultan Malik Shah consolidates power in Persia and the Levant
events.description: In 1075, Malik Shah, the Seljuk Sultan, continued to consolidate his power over the vast Seljuk Empire, which stretched from Anatolia to Persia. Malik Shah's reign (1072–1092) was marked by administrative reforms, the patronage of scholars and poets, and the expansion of Seljuk influence. He was supported by his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, one of the most capable administrators of the medieval Islamic world. Under Malik Shah, the Seljuk Empire reached the height of its power and cultural achievement, becoming a major center of Islamic learning and art.

sources.name: Later Islamic chronicles (e.g., Juvayni's Tarikh-i Jahan-Gusha)

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Malik Shah's consolidation is well-established, but specific events in 1075 are not individually documented in contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1092-001 (Assassination of Nizam al-Mulk and death of Malik Shah)

events.title: Northern Song Dynasty: Wang Anshi appointed Chief Minister
events.description: In 1075, Wang Anshi was appointed Chief Minister (Chancellor) of the Northern Song Dynasty under Emperor Shenzong. Wang Anshi was a brilliant but controversial reformer who sought to strengthen the Song state through radical administrative and economic reforms known as the New Policies (Xin Fa). These included land reform, state monopolies on salt and iron, military reorganization, and new taxation systems. Wang Anshi's appointment marked the beginning of a period of intense political conflict between reformers and conservatives in the Song bureaucracy, a conflict that would define Song politics for decades.
key_figures: Sima Guang (Conservative official, opponent of reforms)

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty, compiled in Yuan Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus on Song political history

events.certainty_note: Wang Anshi's appointment in 1075 is well-documented in Song historical records.

events.title: Normans consolidate control of southern Italy and Sicily
events.description: In 1075, the Norman conquest of southern Italy and Sicily was nearing completion. The Normans, led by Robert Guiscard and his brother Roger I, had been gradually conquering the region from the Byzantine Empire and Arab emirs since the 1050s. By 1075, Norman control was firmly established, and the process of integrating the diverse populations—Greeks, Arabs, Lombards, and Normans—was underway. This Norman conquest would have profound consequences for Mediterranean politics and culture, creating a unique multicultural kingdom that would become the Kingdom of Sicily.
key_figures: Pope Gregory VII (papal ally of the Normans)

sources.name: William of Apulia, Gesta Roberti Wiscardi (near-contemporary chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Norman consolidation is well-established, but specific events in 1075 are not individually highlighted in contemporary sources.

events.title: Seljuk Turks establish the Sultanate of Rum in Anatolia
events.description: In the years following the Battle of Manzikert (1071), the Seljuk Turks established the Sultanate of Rum (Sultanate of Rûm) in Anatolia, with its capital at Nicaea (later moved to Konya). By 1075, the sultanate was firmly established and expanding. The Sultanate of Rum would become one of the most important Seljuk successor states and would dominate Anatolia for nearly two centuries. The establishment of the sultanate represented a fundamental shift in the political and demographic character of Anatolia, from a Byzantine Greek-speaking region to a Turkish-speaking Islamic one.
key_figures: Sulayman I (Founder of the Sultanate of Rum, ruled 1077–1086)
Malik Shah (Seljuk Sultan, overlord of Rum)

sources.name: Anna Komnena, Alexiad (Byzantine chronicle, early 12th century)

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Sultanate of Rum is well-documented in later sources, but the exact date is debated. Some sources place it in 1077, others in 1075 or earlier. The process was gradual rather than a single founding event.
cross_references: 1077-002 (Sulayman I's formal establishment of the sultanate)

disconfirming_evidence: The Dictatus Papae (1075-002) is sometimes presented as a formal papal decree, but modern scholarship suggests it may have been a personal memorandum or working document rather than an official pronouncement. Its exact status and intended audience remain debated. The excommunication of Henry IV (1075-001) is sometimes portrayed as immediately effective, but in reality, it had limited immediate impact outside the papacy's direct sphere of influence; many German bishops and nobles continued to support Henry. The establishment of the Sultanate of Rum (1075-006) is often dated to 1077 or even later; the process was gradual, and there is no single founding moment. Wang Anshi's appointment (1075-004) is sometimes dated to 1074; sources vary on the exact year.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1075 is rich for European events, particularly the Investiture Controversy, which is extensively covered in contemporary chronicles and papal records. However, the sources are heavily weighted toward the perspective of the papacy and the German church; secular and popular perspectives are largely absent. For East Asia, the Song historical records are detailed but were compiled centuries later. For the Islamic world, contemporary sources are sparse; most information comes from later compilations. The Norman conquest of southern Italy is well-documented in contemporary chronicles, but the perspective is heavily weighted toward the Norman conquerors. There is a significant Western bias in the surviving documentation for this year.

graph_edges.note: The excommunication and the Dictatus Papae were issued in the same year as part of Gregory VII's assertion of papal supremacy
Gregory VII's excommunication prompted Henry IV's response at the Synod of Worms
Wang Anshi's appointment led to opposition that would culminate in his resignation
The Seljuk victory at Manzikert enabled the establishment of the Sultanate of Rum
